topic: Reasoning in AI, explained without jargon — what is actually happening under the word 'thinking'
title: When people say AI 'thinks': this is what's actually happening
summary: This breaks down the mechanism behind what gets called AI reasoning, strips away marketing, and notes where we still have no good answers.
listicle: There is no little thinker inside. It does not pause, weigh, or wonder. It guesses the next most appropriate token, every single time.
It can produce correct reasoning outputs the same way you can recite a math proof you memorized before you understood it.
Sometimes it stumbles into correct new steps. We do not yet know reliably when this is happening, versus just pattern matching.
None of this means it isn't useful. It just means "reasoning" is the wrong word for what is occurring.
No one working on this actually has a full explanation. Anyone who tells you otherwise is selling something.
thread: The weirdest unremarked observation: it often lands the right answer before it writes out the reasoning steps.
We are also extremely bad at defining what human reasoning actually is, which makes this whole conversation broken at the start.
Every benchmark test we have built for AI reasoning can be gamed by the exact pattern matching we are trying to test past.
For now, the only honest answer to "does it think?" is "we can't even properly ask that question yet."

criticNotes.note: You never defined what a token even is. You cannot talk about mechanism if you skip the most fundamental building block.
You claimed it can stumble into new correct steps. There is zero replicated, blinded peer reviewed evidence this ever actually occurs.
That last list bullet drifts into snark. You were doing careful curiosity just fine until you slipped into cynicism there.
